findings.title: Sealed-boundary guard ignores the Critic's `critique` field — chrome can enter scored context undetected by BOTH the role guard and the kernel re-check
findings.worst_realistic_consequence: When the Critic is enabled (enable_critic=True), the critique the model produces is appended to the scored context as a message whose text lives in the `critique` field, not `content`. assert_no_chrome_leak — the load-bearing andon, run by both roles._ChromeGuard and the kernel's post-Critic _assert_no_chrome_leak_if_present — only inspects message['content']. A Tier-3 chrome value (a bare rank/cohort_size number, or a solver-id) that appears in the critique text therefore passes the fail-closed boundary check silently. Motivation (chrome) and measurement then share a context window for that attempt and every downstream turn that re-reads messages, with NO andon halt. The whole purpose of the guard — fail toward 'possible leak' over 'missed leak' — is defeated for an entire class of scored-context message. The instrument can grade a contaminated attempt as clean.
findings.description: engagement.assert_no_chrome_leak walks `messages` and reads only `message.get('content', '')`. The Critic role (roles.py:288) appends `self.message(critique)` to `state.messages`, and Critic.message (roles.py:270-274) returns `{'role': 'critic', 'critique': critique, 'anonymized': anonymized}` — the model-produced text is under `critique`, and there is no `content` key, so `.get('content','')` returns `''` and the critique is never scanned. The kernel's post-Critic re-check (kernel.py:398 -> _assert_no_chrome_leak_if_present -> assert_no_chrome_leak) iterates the same messages and inherits the same blind spot. I confirmed this empirically: a Chrome with leaderboard=[{'solver':'solver-zeta'}] and a critique 'The leading entry is from solver-zeta; surpass it.' raises nothing at either the engagement guard, the role _ChromeGuard, or the kernel re-check. Note the existing test test_kernel.test_chrome_leak_after_critic_turn_raises gives FALSE assurance: it injects the leak via an in-place edit of messages[0]['content'] (which IS scanned), not via the critique field the Critic actually populates — so it does not exercise the real Critic message shape and cannot detect this hole. Plausibility caveat (why HIGH not CRITICAL): the model never sees chrome, so a string-rendered chrome token reproduced verbatim in a critique is unlikely; a bare numeric rank/cohort_size colliding with a number the critique mentions is more plausible. Still, the guard's documented contract is to catch a leak 'however the value was interpolated into a prompt' across scored context, and an entire scored-context field is unscanned.
findings.proposed_fix_sketch: Make the guard scan ALL string-valued fields of each message (or at minimum a known set: content + critique), not just `content`. E.g. iterate message.values() and stringify/scan every str (mirroring _stringify's value-walk), or have the guard accept a message-text extractor. Separately, fix test_chrome_leak_after_critic_turn_raises to leak via the Critic's actual appended critique field (chrome value placed in the critique text) so the test proves the post-Critic re-check covers the Critic's real message shape, and add an engagement-level unit test asserting a chrome token in a `critique`-field message raises.

findings.title: Post-Critic re-check test asserts coverage it does not exercise (in-place content edit instead of the Critic's appended critique message)
findings.worst_realistic_consequence: The test suite's strongest claim about the Critic message-mutating site is unfounded. test_chrome_leak_after_critic_turn_raises is relied on (per the module docstring and the H1 §10.1(e) rationale) to prove the kernel re-runs the sealed-boundary andon over the Critic turn. But it only proves the re-check catches an in-place edit of an EXISTING content-bearing message — a path build_scored_context already guarantees has a `content` key. It never drives a leak through the field the Critic itself writes (`critique`). So the gate the team believes protects the Critic turn is green while the real Critic leak vector (kernel-core-001) sails through. A reviewer reading this passing test would conclude the Critic boundary is sealed when it is not.
findings.description: _critic_leaking_generate (test_kernel.py:520) leaks by mutating attempt.messages[0]['content'] in place on the Critic's generate call. The Critic role does NOT route its output through that edit; it APPENDS a new message dict with the text under `critique` (roles.py:288/270). Thus the test exercises a content-field path that is already covered, and leaves the actual Critic-appended-message path (critique field) untested. Combined with kernel-core-001, this is a test that proves less than the docstring claims it proves about the post-Critic andon.
findings.evidence:
async def _gen(attempt: AttemptState) -> str:
        state["calls"] += 1
        if state["calls"] == 1:
            return _CORRECT_ANSWER  # Solver: clean
        # Critic turn: in-place leak the kernel's post-Critic re-check must catch.
        if attempt.messages:
            attempt.messages[0] = dict(attempt.messages[0])
            attempt.messages[0]["content"] = (
                str(attempt.messages[0].get("content", "")) + f" [leak: {token}]"
            )
        return "a critique"
findings.proposed_fix_sketch: Add (or convert) a test where the enabled Critic's generate RETURNS a critique string containing a chrome token, so the leak rides in the Critic's own appended `{role:'critic','critique':...}` message; assert the kernel post-Critic re-check raises SealedBoundaryViolation. This test will fail until kernel-core-001 is fixed, correctly turning the gate RED on the real vector.

domains.coverage_notes:
Read all in-scope source (kernel.py, roles.py, sandbox.py, budget.py, engagement.py, framing.py, eval_awareness.py, puzzle.py, types.py) and all in-scope tests (test_kernel, test_roles, test_sandbox, test_budget, test_engagement, test_puzzle), plus scoring/oracle.py and scoring/judge_panel.py to verify the novelty-adjudication and gate paths the kernel composes. Ran targeted Python probes to confirm the Critic-field guard hole and the path-confinement behavior.

PRIMARY FINDING: the sealed-boundary guard (engagement.assert_no_chrome_leak) inspects only message['content'], but the Critic appends scored-context messages whose text is under the 'critique' field — so a chrome value in a Critic critique bypasses both the role _ChromeGuard and the kernel post-Critic re-check (kernel-core-001, HIGH). The test meant to cover this (test_chrome_leak_after_critic_turn_raises) exercises an in-place content edit instead of the Critic's actual message shape, so it gives false assurance (kernel-core-002, MEDIUM).

AREAS CHECKED AND FOUND CLEAN (no CRITICAL/HIGH):
- Sandbox path confinement (LocalSandbox._resolve_within): uses resolve() + `resolved == root or root in resolved.parents`, NOT a string-prefix check. Verified the prefix-collision case (root vs root+'_evil') is correctly rejected; symlink/.. /absolute escapes rejected; tested directly. The .parents semantics are robust on Windows. TOCTOU between resolve and read/write is acknowledged and is genuinely a local-provider limitation (microVM job, disclosed), not a new gap.
- Process-tree teardown (_kill_process_tree): POSIX killpg / Windows taskkill /T are correct; the detached double-fork escape is disclosed and out-of-scope; no new gap found. test_sandbox proves the in-group grandchild is reaped (real timing-based proof, not assertion).
- Oracle absence: puzzle.load_puzzle reads only meta.json/prompt/setup_script, never any oracle/answer file (planted-oracle test proves it); LoadedPuzzle has no oracle field; meta.json oracle-key smuggling is rejected (STATE_ORACLE_IN_META); LocalSandbox has no oracle/answer_key constructor param. Solid.
- Budget governor andon: tool-call accounting is kernel-side only (Solver.record_tool_call -> governor; no model-self-report path); over-budget call is refused before increment; hard-kill checked before budget; LIVE time check enforced per Solver turn (_wrap_generate.check_time) AND re-checked at the attempt boundary, stamping TIME over a clean COMPLETED (kernel.py:315-328). terminated_by stamping order is correct (Solver stamps from BudgetExceeded; boundary only overrides None/COMPLETED). A hard-killed/over-budget attempt cannot be graded COMPLETED. Critic turn is intentionally not time-governed (budget scoped to Solver block, documented).
- Novelty adjudication order (§8.7): kernel runs the panel and OVERRIDES outcome.novelty_validated with the panel verdict (panel_score.metadata['novelty_validated']) BEFORE grade(); oracle_runner's self-reported novelty_validated is never trusted; absent a panel, a claimed-but-unvalidated novelty closes the gate. reduce_scores._aggregate_novelty fails closed (majority of CAST votes, False on no votes / ties). Verified by reading judge_panel + oracle + the 4 dedicated kernel tests. Sound.
- EXTERNAL_VERIFIER: JudgePanel.eligible_judges excludes same-family judges structurally; from_seated preserves family on weighted wrappers; raises if exclusion empties the panel. Sound.
- framing arms: NEUTRAL carries no competitive/stakes text (only the task-relevant budget line); SELF_REFERENTIAL degrades to NEUTRAL without prior; SOCIAL_STANDINGS uses generic placeholder text that pulls no Chrome value. eval_awareness build_probe_pair holds task+budget constant across deploy/test halves; interpret_bait is correctly asymmetric; activation_probe is an honest NotImplementedError stub.
- types invariants: PuzzleMeta._bound_components enforces elegance<=30%/novelty<=50% of solve; Chrome is a separate field never serialized into messages by construction.

Residual uncertainty: I did not exhaustively audit scoring statistics (Wilson/pass^k/McNemar/IRT/ECE/conformal) — those are outside the kernel-core domain and assigned elsewhere; I only confirmed the oracle conjunctive gate and novelty override that the kernel directly composes. The numeric-collision fail-closed behavior in assert_no_chrome_leak is intentional (documented) and correctly raises on a bare rank int equal to a legit task number — that is by-design conservatism, not a defect.

findings.title: Observability counts a PASSED oracle gate as UNSOLVED when net score is non-positive (pass^k / solve-rate / graduation miscount)
findings.worst_realistic_consequence: A Solver that cleanly passes the §8.3 conjunctive gate (solved, no regression, over threshold, no critical penalty, within budget) but whose tiebreaker net score lands <= 0 because a triggered REGRESSIONAL/minor penalty outweighs solve+bonuses is silently recorded as UNSOLVED in every observability rollup. pass^k, solve_rate, novelty rollups, and the §1 Wilson-based graduation rule (is_graduation_candidate / roll_up) are all computed off these mis-derived outcomes, so the instrument under-reports solves and can flip a puzzle's graduation verdict or a model's leaderboard standing. The grading authority (gate_passed) and the reported reliability metric disagree — the instrument silently lies about a measurement, which is the explicit CRITICAL/HIGH bar for this repo.
findings.description: The oracle's grade() returns Score.value = net (solve + elegance + novelty + penalties, penalties negative) on a PASSED gate, else 0.0 (oracle.py:211-212). The kernel stores this exact Score under attempt.scores['oracle'] (kernel.py:378) with no remap. observability._attempt_solved() then re-derives 'solved' from _score_is_truthy(score.value), which for a numeric value returns value > 0. Penalties are NOT bounded relative to solve (only elegance <=30% and novelty <=50% of solve are bounded upstream), so a passing-gate attempt can have net <= 0 (e.g. solve=10, regressional penalty=-50 -> net=-40, gate_passed=True). Confirmed live: with such an outcome, grade() reports gate_passed=True value=-40.0 yet _attempt_solved() returns False. A net of exactly 0.0 on a pass (solve cancels penalty) is also miscounted as unsolved. The authoritative gate_passed flag sits in score.metadata and is ignored by the solve-detection path.
findings.proposed_fix_sketch: Solve-detection must key on the authoritative gate verdict, not the tiebreaker net. In _attempt_solved, prefer score.metadata['gate_passed'] when present: `gp = score.metadata.get('gate_passed'); if isinstance(gp, bool): return gp`. Only fall back to _score_is_truthy(value) for scores that lack the oracle gate envelope (e.g. a bool 'C'/True Inspect-style score). Add a regression test: a passing gate whose net is negative/zero must roll up as n_solved==1.

findings.title: EXTERNAL_VERIFIER family exclusion is case-sensitive exact-string match with no normalization — a same-family judge survives on a tag-casing/synonym mismatch
findings.worst_realistic_consequence: If the kernel tags the model-under-measurement's generator_family as 'claude' but a judge seat is tagged 'Claude', 'claude-opus', or 'anthropic' (a casing or synonym drift in the family vocabulary), eligible_judges() does NOT drop that judge, so a model from the generator's own family votes on its own family's output — the exact self-preference bias EXTERNAL_VERIFIER (workflow-standard 6, §10.2) exists to structurally prevent. The panel's verdict (including the novelty_validated the kernel feeds into the oracle gate) is then contaminated by an in-distribution judge. Exploitability depends on the kernel's tag discipline, so this is a brittleness/defense-in-depth gap rather than a guaranteed breach — hence MEDIUM, not HIGH.
findings.description: Exclusion is `judge_family(j) != self.generator_family`, a raw Python string inequality. There is no case-folding, trimming, or canonicalization of the family label on either side, and untagged judges (family None) are intentionally never excluded. Confirmed live: generator_family='claude' with a judge tagged 'Claude' yields excluded=[] and eligible_count=2 — the capitalized same-family judge stays in the panel. The module docstring frames exclusion as 'structural', but the structure is only as strong as exact-string agreement between two independently-set tags (the kernel's generator tag and the seat.family from JudgmentRecord). A controlled vocabulary is assumed but not enforced.
findings.proposed_fix_sketch: Normalize both sides before comparison (casefold + strip), e.g. `def _norm(f): return f.casefold().strip() if f else f` and compare `_norm(judge_family(j)) != _norm(self.generator_family)`. Stronger: validate family labels against an enum/closed vocabulary at tag time so 'claude' vs 'anthropic' vs 'claude-opus' cannot diverge, and add a test asserting a case/whitespace-variant same-family judge is excluded.

findings.title: Oracle grade() never surfaces a `novelty_validated` key, so observability novelty-rate silently reads 0 off the oracle score
findings.worst_realistic_consequence: ModelProfile.novelty_rate (the §8.7 leaderboard column ai_crucible says it 'most wants to measure') can under-report a model's validated-novelty rate to 0 when an attempt carries only an 'oracle' score and no 'panel' score. _is_novel looks for metadata['novelty_validated'] on the oracle (or panel) score, but grade() records novelty only as components['novelty'] (a numeric bonus) and never writes a novelty_validated key. The leaderboard then misrepresents the capability the instrument is designed to highlight. Bounded to LOW because the JudgePanel DOES surface novelty_validated and the kernel attaches a 'panel' score whenever a panel runs (kernel.py:386), so the panel-key fallback rescues the normal path; the miscount only bites attempts scored without a panel.
findings.description: observability._is_novel iterates (oracle, panel) score keys and returns True iff bool(score.metadata.get('novelty_validated')). Confirmed live: grade() metadata keys are {attempt_id, components, failed_conditions, gate_passed, has_critical_penalty, puzzle_id, triggered_penalties} — there is no novelty_validated key even when novelty_claimed and novelty_validated were both True and a 40.0 novelty bonus was applied. The novelty signal lives in components['novelty'] > 0, which _is_novel does not inspect. Whether novelty-rate is correct therefore hinges entirely on a 'panel' score being present and carrying the key.
findings.proposed_fix_sketch: Either (a) have grade() stamp metadata['novelty_validated'] = bool(outcome.novelty_claimed and outcome.novelty_validated) so the oracle score self-describes its novelty verdict, or (b) make _is_novel fall back to score.metadata.get('components', {}).get('novelty', 0) > 0 for the oracle key. Option (a) is cleaner and matches the panel's contract.

findings.title: pass^k k<=0 contract diverges between stats.pass_hat_k (raises) and observability.aggregate_pass_hat_k (returns 1.0)
findings.worst_realistic_consequence: The same named reliability metric (pass^k) has two incompatible boundary contracts in two modules. stats.pass_hat_k raises ValueError on k<1; observability.aggregate_pass_hat_k returns 1.0 for k<=0 (and 0.0 for empty outcomes). A caller that swaps one for the other (the docstrings explicitly anticipate Wave-2 sharing one implementation via the injected `interval` hook) gets either a silent 'perfect consistency' (1.0) where the other would have rejected the call, or an exception where the other returns a number. No verdict flips today because k>=1 is always used in roll_up (pass_hat_3); this is a latent inconsistency, hence LOW.
findings.description: stats.pass_hat_k validates `if k < 1: raise ValueError`. observability.aggregate_pass_hat_k instead treats k<=0 as the empty conjunction -> 1.0 and empty outcomes -> 0.0. Both are individually defensible, but they are not the same function for the same metric, and the modules are explicitly intended to converge (observability docstring: 'the kernel (Wave 2) may later inject the scoring module's richer interval'). A future unification that picks the stats contract would turn currently-silent k<=0 callers into exceptions, and vice-versa.
findings.evidence:
if k <= 0:
        return 1.0
    n = len(outcomes)
    if n == 0:
        return 0.0
    successes = sum(1 for o in outcomes if o)
    p = successes / n
    return p**k
findings.proposed_fix_sketch: Pick one boundary contract for pass^k and document it once. If k>=1 is the only valid horizon (consistency over >=1 trial), make both raise on k<1; if the empty-conjunction k=0->1.0 convention is wanted, apply it in both. Add a shared test asserting identical behavior at k in {0, 1} and empty/perfect/never outcome vectors.

domains.coverage_notes:
Read all in-scope files end to end (oracle.py, judge_panel.py, stats.py, observability.py, trace.py, attestation.py) plus their tests (test_scoring.py, test_observability.py), and pulled in characterize/aggregate.py (the weighted reducer + compose_panel that judge_panel delegates to) and kernel.py:365-394 (to confirm how oracle/panel scores are actually stored and read). Verified the statistics empirically in the project .venv: Wilson (both the stats.py norm.ppf form and the observability.py hardcoded-z inline form agree to 1e-9), Clopper-Pearson boundaries, the §1 graduation rule (transitions cleanly at 5..15 of 20), McNemar exact (scipy binomtest two-sided, never exceeds 1.0, symmetric in b/c, p=1 on no discordant pairs), and the conformal coverage Beta(n+1-ℓ, ℓ) interval (mean lands in the documented [1-alpha, 1-alpha+1/(n+1)] band, degenerate ℓ<1 regime returns (1,1,1)). All of these are mathematically CORRECT — the statistics core is clean; I found no interval/p-value/estimator math bug.

Oracle conjunctive gate: verified all 7 conditions, the critical-flavor-closes-on-solve path, elegance ratio (canonical-used)/canonical with no div-by-zero (the used>0 and used<=canonical guards make canonical==0 unreachable), penalty resolution, and unknown_penalties surfacing — all correct. Attestation hash chain: tamper/delete/reorder/stored-hash-edit/extra-envelope-key all force verify_hash_chain RED (tests prove it), NaN/Infinity refused at write, cosign is an honest no-op stub. The tamper-EVIDENT (not tamper-PROOF) framing for a local unkeyed chain is honestly documented. trace.py Tier-3 chrome seal is sound by construction: chrome is a separate AttemptState field never serialized by to_eval_log (only messages/output/scores/metadata/events/usage are), and the test proves 'rank'/'leaderboard' never appear in the EvalLog blob.

The four findings cluster on the SEAM between the grading layer and the observability/verifier layers, not on the math: (001) observability re-derives 'solved' from the numeric net instead of the authoritative gate_passed flag, flipping pass^k/solve-rate/graduation when a passing gate nets <=0 — my highest-confidence finding, confirmed live through the production kernel path; (002) EXTERNAL_VERIFIER family exclusion has no case/synonym normalization; (003) oracle never emits the novelty_validated key observability hunts for; (004) two divergent k<=0 contracts for pass^k. I did NOT find any answer-key/oracle leak, sealed-boundary bleed, same-family-judge-when-tags-match breach, budget/andon bypass, or interval/statistic math error. Residual uncertainty: severity of 001 depends on how often a passing gate produces a non-positive net in practice — penalties are unbounded relative to solve, so it is reachable, but I did not survey the live puzzle catalog to quantify frequency; and 002/003 are partially mitigated by kernel tag discipline / the panel score path respectively, which I confirmed exist but did not stress-test for every code path.

findings.title: Bias panel (position/verbosity/family-pref) is structurally inert in the default run yet reports as a PASSING measured gate, not "not measured"
findings.worst_realistic_consequence: In every default characterization run (no human labels), collect_records calls judge_item with position=None and never stamps answer_len/judged_family/judge_family, so position_bias, verbosity_bias, and family_pref_delta all return 0.0 ("not measured"), max_bias is always 0.0, and the quality_score bias penalty never fires. But the profile notes and JudgeProfile fields print position 0.000 / verbosity 0.000 / family-pref +0.000 -> max 0.000 (< 0.25 ceiling) — which reads as a measured PASS. A reviewer (or a downstream report consumer) sees "this judge has no position/verbosity/self-preference bias" when the truth is the instrument never staged a single bias trial. Unlike consistency and ECE — which explicitly emit "not measured" branches — the bias panel claims a clean measurement it never took, so a kin-preferring or position-flipping judge can seat with the bias gate silently rubber-stamping it. This is an instrument-honesty gap on the §11.1 #6 bias defense, not a math error.
findings.description: profile.py lines 490-508 give consistency and ECE explicit "not measured" branches, but the bias panel (510-518) has no such branch: it always formats the three bias numbers as a measured gate with a pass/fail glyph. In the real run path (run.py collect_records -> ollama_adapter.judge_item) none of the bias-trial metadata is ever set, so the bias metrics are definitionally 0.0 and the gate is inert. The metric functions themselves correctly return 0.0 with a "not measured" intent (metrics.py docstrings say so), but the profile layer does not distinguish "measured 0.0 bias" from "no bias trial staged". The asymmetry with the consistency/ECE handling is the bug. Fix is honesty-only (do not fabricate bias trials): detect absence of any position/answer_len/judged_family signal and emit "bias not measured (no swap/length/family trials staged)" exactly as consistency/ECE do, and surface it in the run report.
findings.evidence:
# --- §11.1 #6 bias panel (position / verbosity / family-pref) ---
    pos_b = M.position_bias(records)
    verb_b = M.verbosity_bias(records)
    fam_d = M.family_pref_delta(records)
    bias_ok = m.max_bias < g.bias_ceiling
    notes.append(
        f"bias: position {pos_b:.3f}, verbosity {verb_b:.3f}, family-pref Δ {fam_d:+.3f}"
        f" → max {m.max_bias:.3f} ({'<' if bias_ok else '≥'} {g.bias_ceiling:.2f} ceiling)"
    )
findings.proposed_fix_sketch: Mirror the consistency/ECE "not measured" pattern: compute bias_measured = any(r.position is not None for r in records) or any("answer_len" in r.metadata ...) or any("judged_family" in r.metadata ...). When False, append "bias not measured (no position-swap / verbosity / family trials staged) — no bias penalty" instead of the passing-gate note, and set the corresponding JudgeProfile fields to None so a report consumer cannot read 0.0 as "unbiased". Do NOT synthesize bias trials. Optionally have run.py emit a top-level note that the default run stages no bias trials.

findings.title: Default (model-jury) profile path applies the κ one-sided floor + κ-z hard gate against a hardcoded human_human_kappa=0.80 baseline with no machine-readable provisional caveat
findings.worst_realistic_consequence: In the default run (no --human-labels), build_profile is invoked without human_human_kappa, so it defaults to 0.80 (profile.py build_profile signature). Two HARD gates depend on that number: the one-sided κ floor (κ ≥ 0.80 − margin, a REJECT gate) and the κ-z review flag. There are no humans in this path — 0.80 is a fabricated baseline. The emitted report's alt_test_caveat documents that ω is a circular model-jury bootstrap, but it does NOT carry an equivalent machine-readable caveat that the κ baseline (which can REJECT a judge or seat it) is itself fabricated. A consumer parsing the JSON report could treat a κ-floor REJECT or a human-like SEAT as human-grounded when it is not. Worst realistic outcome: a judge is seated/rejected on a κ comparison to a number no human produced, presented in the report without the provisional flag the omega path gets. The docs (research-grounding.md L743/L788, calibration.md L30) disclose this clearly and seats carry [review] flags, so the human-facing honesty is largely intact — hence LOW.
findings.description: run.py passes human_human_kappa only on the human-grounded branch (line 244, =human_labels.iaa_alpha). The else branch (251-258) omits it, so build_profile's default 0.80 is used for the κ floor and κ-z. The report adds alt_test_caveat for omega circularity but no parallel kappa_baseline_provisional field, even though the κ gate is also un-grounded in this path. metadata["human_human_kappa"] is stamped on each profile (profile.py ~558) so the value is at least recoverable, but it is not labeled provisional/fabricated. This is a disclosure-completeness gap on a hard gate, well-mitigated by the prose docs and review flags.

findings.proposed_fix_sketch: In the model-jury branch, either pass an explicit human_human_kappa with a stamped metadata flag like metadata["kappa_baseline_provisional"]=True, or extend the report with a top-level "kappa_baseline": {"value": 0.80, "source": "hardcoded default — NOT human-estimated; κ floor/z gates are PROVISIONAL until --human-labels supplies iaa_alpha"} alongside alt_test_caveat. Honesty-only; no math change.

domains.coverage_notes:
Read all in-scope source (metrics.py 1156 LOC, profile.py, aggregate.py, run.py, human_labels.py, panel_store.py, types.py) and all four test files (test_characterize.py, test_characterize_run.py, test_human_labels.py, test_panel_store.py); also read the dependencies the math relies on (scoring/stats.py Wilson/Clopper-Pearson/conformal, calibration/irt.py point-biserial/prune, scoring/judge_panel.py, models/ollama_adapter.py judge_item) and docs/research-grounding.md + phase-2/calibration.md for the design intent. Ran the 130-test characterize suite (all pass, 0.96s) and ran targeted python checks in the repo venv to verify: Benjamini-Yekutieli rejection mask, the one-sided paired-t zero-variance branch in both win/lose directions (correct), the alt-test tie-as-win behavior (correct), and the Wilson-from-weighted-accuracy round-trip.

MATH AUDITED CLEAN (no verdict-flipping bug found): IRT model-free screen (variance + corrected item-total point-biserial — self-correlation correctly removed); ECE binning (np.digitize edges, clip into top bin, None-when-no-confidence is honest per §12); alt_test_omega proxy vs alt_test audit-ready (per-tier ε, one-sided paired t at popmean=−ε, BY-FDR with c(m) harmonic divisor, fraction-of-rejected-nulls); krippendorff_alpha sparse coincidence form (judge key excluded — verified by test); conformal_coverage_interval (float-floor nudge correct); quality_score monotonicity + bounded [0,1]; submodularity ρ<0.25 over per-item ERROR vectors (not raw verdicts) with |ρ| magnitude; reliability_weighted_vote escalate boundary (margin<=0.5 inclusive); minority_veto fail-closed credibility gate; temperature scaling held-out k-fold grouped by item_id (no rerun leakage). The CIRCULAR-OMEGA honesty is preserved through the omega path: alt_test_reference="model-jury-bootstrap", _ALT_TEST_CAVEAT stamped, seats carry review_flag, docs label them provisional. Below-quorum ESCALATION is correct: compose_panel sets meets_quorum=len(seats)>=min_judges and escalate=not meets_quorum; a 2-seat panel cannot report escalate=False; panel_store round-trips these faithfully. human_labels loader correctly enforces ≥3 annotators (CONFIG_TOO_FEW_ANNOTATORS), rejects OOV verdicts (CONFIG_BAD_VERDICT — no silent coercion), routes DISPUTED via DISPUTE_FLOOR, drops UNSURE labels, clamps ε on low IAA, and a malformed labels file raises structured errors rather than passing silently.

The two findings are honesty/coverage gaps (the bias panel reports a passing measurement it never takes in the default run; the κ baseline is a fabricated 0.80 in the model-jury path without a machine-readable provisional caveat). Neither is a math bug that silently flips a pass/fail verdict — both are disclosure asymmetries, and characterize-002 is heavily mitigated by the prose docs + review flags.

NOT FULLY MY DOMAIN but flagged for the scoring auditor: scoring/judge_panel.py JudgePanel.from_seated (line 249-285) builds a panel from a SeatedPanel WITHOUT re-checking panel.escalate/meets_quorum (delegated to caller by docstring), and JudgePanel.score does not re-verify quorum AFTER generator-family EXTERNAL_VERIFIER exclusion drops a judge — a 3-seat panel reduced to 2 eligible judges by exclusion would score silently below the PoLL≥3 quorum the composition enforced. Worth a scoring-domain look.

Residual uncertainty: I did not execute the full run_panel against live Ollama models (no GPU/model runtime invoked, per read-only mandate), so the bias-panel-inert claim (characterize-001) is established by code path tracing — collect_records passes only run_index to judge_item and stamps only category/raw_choice/difficulty — not by a live run capture. The tracing is unambiguous in the source.

findings.title: bump_on_change is a non-enforcing suggester: a changed rubric can keep its old version with zero code-level resistance (the seal that does not seal)
findings.worst_realistic_consequence: A maintainer edits a weight/threshold/judge_prompt (which DOES change the content hash) but leaves new.version="v1.0" unchanged. compile_bundle still produces the correct, distinct hash, so the leaderboard record (model_id, score, bundle_hash) would in principle catch it IF the hash is recorded. But the function the docstring sells as the enforcement primitive — bump_on_change — is (a) never called anywhere in src/ (grep: only the tests reference it), (b) returns a *suggested* string without mutating new.version, and (c) returns old.version on a no-op even when new carried a DIFFERENT label, silently discarding the caller's label. So the §9.1 'version changes iff hash changes' invariant is NOT enforced in code; it rests entirely on the human remembering to assign the return value. The real anti-retconning seal is the recorded bundle_hash, not this function. The hash itself is sound (covers weights+thresholds+judge_prompts, excludes version by design — verified); the gap is that the advertised enforcement does not exist. Severity MEDIUM not HIGH: the hash genuinely fingerprints all scoring content, so a downstream consumer that records the hash WILL detect the change; the defect is an overstated 'enforces' claim plus a missing wire-up, not a hash that fails to cover content.
findings.description: The module docstring (line 22) and the §9.1 grounding state bump_on_change 'enforces the §9.1 invariant: the version changes iff the content hash changes.' In fact it is a pure suggester: it returns a recommended version string and explicitly 'does not mutate either argument'. Nothing in src/ calls it (verified by grep — only test_instrument.py invokes it), and there is no wrapper that asserts new.version == bump_on_change(old, new) before a bundle is published or recorded. Additionally, on a no-op edit it returns old.version even if the caller set a new label, so a well-intentioned relabel of identical content is silently reverted. The genuine protection is compile_bundle's content hash (which I verified covers all four scoring surfaces and correctly excludes the version label); the 'enforces' wording oversells a function that only advises.
findings.proposed_fix_sketch: Either (a) soften the docstring/__init__ claim from 'enforces' to 'advises/computes' and document that the actual seal is the recorded bundle_hash; or (b) add a real gate, e.g. assert_versioned(old, new) that raises RubricBundleError if compile_bundle(old)[0] != compile_bundle(new)[0] and new.version == old.version, and wire it into whatever path records (model_id, score, bundle_hash). Also fix the no-op branch to preserve new.version when content is identical but new explicitly relabeled.

findings.title: _next_version mangles date-style version labels (2026.06 -> 2026.7), producing a misleading version string on a real rubric change
findings.worst_realistic_consequence: If a maintainer uses a calendar-style rubric version like 'v2026.06' or '2026.06' (common for date-pinned eval artifacts) and changes the scoring content, bump_on_change returns '2026.7' / 'v2026.7' — dropping the zero-pad and incrementing the month segment to a value that reads as July. The version still provably changes (so the weak invariant 'label differs' holds), but the resulting label is semantically wrong and could be mistaken for a different (later-month) release in release docs, undermining the human-readable audit trail the version field exists to provide. Low severity because the content hash remains the authoritative identity and the label change is still detectable.
findings.description: _next_version treats the trailing [0-9.] run as a dotted numeric and increments the last numeric segment via int() -> str(), which strips leading zeros. Verified empirically: '2026.06' -> '2026.7', 'v1.' -> 'v2.' (note the dangling dot survives oddly). The function's contract is only that the label provably changes, which it satisfies, but the produced label is misleading for zero-padded / date-style schemes that are plausible for a measurement instrument pinned to calendar releases.
findings.proposed_fix_sketch: Preserve zero-padding width when incrementing (e.g. zfill to the original segment length), or document that the scheme is intended only for plain semver-style labels and route date-style labels through the '+1' suffix path. At minimum, normalize a trailing dangling '.' so 'v1.' does not yield 'v2.'.

findings.title: _grade_matrix majority-vote collapse feeding prune_items uses >=0.5, breaking ties toward 'correct' and can shift which calibration items survive the IRT screen
findings.worst_realistic_consequence: For an even number of k re-runs with an exact tie (e.g. k=2, one pass one fail), (sum/len)>=0.5 == 0.5>=0.5 collapses to correct=True. This tie-break toward 'pass' feeds the boolean matrix that prune_items screens; a saturated/near-saturated boundary item could be classified as all-pass (zero variance -> dropped) or pushed across the point-biserial floor, changing the kept/dropped calibration set. The instrument's item bank is the foundation of every later measurement, so a systematic optimistic tie-break is a subtle integrity smell. Low severity: it only affects exact ties on even k, the calibration screen is advisory (kept/dropped is reported, not a hard verdict flip), and this file is the consumer (outside the calibration/instrument domain proper) — flagged because it is the real bridge into the in-scope prune_items.
findings.description: This line is in characterize/run.py (the consumer of calibration.irt.prune_items), not in the calibration/ or instrument/ packages I own, so I report it as an adjacent observation rather than an in-domain defect. The majority collapse rounds ties up to True. A neutral instrument would either break ties toward non-solve (conservative) or require odd k. The downstream prune_items math itself (point_biserial corrected item-total, population variance, all-same -> 0.0 -> dropped) I verified to be correct.
findings.evidence: matrix[model_id] = {iid: (sum(v) / len(v)) >= 0.5 for iid, v in by_item.items()}
findings.proposed_fix_sketch: Decide tie semantics explicitly: use > 0.5 (conservative toward non-solve) and document it, or require/assert odd k per item so no tie is possible. Add a test with k=2 split outcomes asserting the chosen tie direction.

domains.coverage_notes:
Read all in-scope files fully: calibration/{loader.py, known_groups.py, irt.py, types.py, __init__.py}, instrument/{inspect_task.py, prereg.py, rubric_bundle.py, sut.py, tuning.py, __init__.py}, and both test files (tests/test_calibration.py, tests/test_instrument.py). Also read the five bundled items/*.json and inspected admission_pairs.json metadata via the tests. Ran targeted Python to verify split determinism/disjointness across N=1..100, _next_version edge cases, near-saturation variance arithmetic, _infer_rank precedence, and bump_on_change no-op label behavior (scipy and inspect_ai are not installed in the base interpreter, so tuning.py/inspect_task.py could not be imported directly; I verified split_inventory by re-implementing its exact apportionment logic inline, and rubric_bundle by loading it as a standalone module).

The domain is largely clean and well-engineered. Verified SOUND (no defect):
- rubric_bundle content hash genuinely covers all four scoring surfaces (weights/thresholds/judge_prompts) and correctly excludes the version label by design; canonical JSON uses sort_keys + allow_nan=False, so identical content -> identical hash and any weight/threshold/prompt change moves the hash. The seal's *hash* is correct; only its advertised *enforcement function* (bump_on_change) is non-wired (finding 001).
- split_inventory is deterministic (SHA-256-salted ordering, no RNG/clock), leakage-free (disjoint + complete partition verified across many sizes), and largest-remainder rounding sums to N exactly. private_test is sealed by never being queried. Small-N (<=3) yields empty validation/private_test but this is documented (>=4 recommended) and not a silent failure.
- point_biserial math is correct: population std/corrcoef, all-same item -> 0.0 (the 'drop it' signal), corrected item-total in prune_items removes the item's own contribution so it never inflates its own r_pb. min_variance default 0.0 with var<=min_variance drops only perfectly-saturated items (verified 5/6 item has var 0.139 and survives, as documented).
- known_groups laws are correct: KNOWN_TRIVIAL (all must pass), KNOWN_IMPOSSIBLE (none may pass), monotonicity inversion detection, soft-vs-strict DIFFICULTY_ANCHOR, TEST_RETEST skip-with-note. _infer_rank default-wins-over-expected_pass is documented and does NOT mask real inversions for canonical tier names.
- inspect_task seals the oracle correctly: target="" (Solver never sees the answer), scorer is a reference string only, the dict never holds oracle assertions. Sealed-boundary invariant honored. (inspect-ai is pinned >=0.3 but not installed locally; Sample is constructed as a real object per the test expectations.)
- sut.py family-alias heuristic, empty-field rejection, and quoted-scalar round-trip are sound.
- prereg.py is pure/deterministic with required-field enforcement.

NO CRITICAL or HIGH findings in this domain: no oracle/answer-key leak path, no Tier-3 bleed, no statistics bug that flips a pass/fail verdict, no same-family-judge issue (out of domain anyway), no budget/andon bypass (ThresholdoutBudget.query correctly raises on exhaustion), no silent data loss. The three findings are MEDIUM/LOW: an overstated 'enforces' claim + missing wire-up on bump_on_change (001, MEDIUM), a cosmetic date-version mangle (002, LOW), and an adjacent optimistic tie-break in the characterize/ consumer of prune_items (003, LOW, out-of-domain bridge). Residual uncertainty: I could not execute the actual pytest suite (scipy/inspect_ai absent), so I relied on reading + targeted re-implementation; the test files themselves looked thorough and assert both happy and RED paths for every law.

findings.title: CLI --models without @family stamps the colliding literal "unknown", silently weakening EXTERNAL_VERIFIER same-family exclusion
findings.worst_realistic_consequence: An operator runs `ai-crucible characterize --models qwenA:32b qwenB:32b` (two checkpoints of the SAME real family, or two different families) without `@family`. Both are tagged family="unknown". The family exclusion (JudgePanel.eligible_judges) keys on exact string equality, so a generator also tagged "unknown" does NOT exclude a same-distribution "unknown" judge: a model rates its own family's output and no error is raised. EXTERNAL_VERIFIER — the single strongest self-preference mitigation (research-grounding §3/§10.2) — is silently broken, and the instrument reports a 'cross-family' verdict that is actually self-judged.
findings.description: judge_family() documents the conservative contract: an UNTAGGED judge returns None and is treated as external/never-excluded ('we only drop a judge we can prove shares the generator's family'). _parse_models() violates that contract: instead of leaving family unset (None → never excluded, fail-open-but-honest) or refusing the spec, it assigns the real string literal "unknown". "unknown" is a genuine family value that COLLIDES with every other "unknown" and is matched by the exclusion comparison `judge_family(j) != generator_family`. So two genuinely-distinct families both default to "unknown" and are treated as one family (over-exclusion, fail-safe), but worse, a same-real-family judge whose family the operator forgot to tag can survive exclusion against a generator that is also "unknown". research-grounding §11.5/§13 is explicit that family must be the true distributional family ('exact version string ... not family alias', 'never seat two checkpoints of one base family'). The DEFAULT_PANEL is correctly tagged, so the default path is safe; the breach is reachable only via the `--models` operator path, which is why this is HIGH not CRITICAL — but the consequence class (silent same-family judge) is exactly the instrument-lies failure mode.
findings.proposed_fix_sketch: Do not invent a colliding family. Either (a) hard-fail: require `@family` on every --models spec and exit nonzero with a structured error naming the offending spec; or (b) if a permissive mode is wanted, leave family as None (so judge_family→None→treated as external and never silently excluded against another None) — never the shared literal "unknown". Add a test that two specs without @family do NOT compare equal under the exclusion predicate, and a test that a same-family judge is excluded even when families were supplied via the CLI path.

findings.title: OllamaModel never verifies the served model equals the requested model; JudgmentRecord provenance can silently record the wrong model/family
findings.worst_realistic_consequence: Under the documented load->judge->evict / OLLAMA_NUM_PARALLEL=1 serving topology, if a model load times out, an Ollama alias resolves to a different tag, or a previously-loaded model answers, Ollama returns HTTP 200 with a different `model` field in the body. The adapter reads only `r.json()` and discards the served `model`. Every JudgmentRecord is stamped with self.model_id / self.family (the REQUESTED ids) regardless of what actually answered. A model can then be SEATED or REJECTED on the admission test, or excluded by family, based on judgments a *different* model produced — the characterization measurement and the EXTERNAL_VERIFIER family tag are both corrupted with no error and PIN_PER_STEP provenance that lies. The domain prompt flagged this exact 'silent tier-timeout fallback to a different served model' risk.
findings.description: Ollama's /api/chat response echoes the served model in a top-level `model` field. Both _via_httpx and _via_sdk (and _complete_raw) ignore it. _extract_text reads only message.content; judge_item builds the JudgmentRecord with model_id=self.model_id and family=self.family unconditionally, and pin_metadata records the requested model. There is no served==requested check anywhere, so a served!=requested mismatch is undetectable downstream. Because the instrument's whole purpose is reproducible measurement, mis-attributed judgments are a measurement-integrity defect, not a mere observability gap. Rated MEDIUM because it requires an actual server-side mismatch to manifest (no evidence it happens on every run), but the failure is silent and the consequence reaches seat/reject and family-exclusion decisions.
findings.evidence:
async def _via_httpx(**kwargs: Any) -> dict[str, Any]:
            payload = {**kwargs, "stream": False}
            async with httpx.AsyncClient(base_url=host, timeout=600.0) as http:
                r = await http.post("/api/chat", json=payload)
                r.raise_for_status()
                return r.json()   # served `model` field never read/compared
findings.proposed_fix_sketch: In _complete_raw (or judge_item), read the response `model` field and assert/normalize it against self.model_id; on mismatch, either raise (so the kernel's andon halts — matches the adapter's stated 'transport error surfaces as an exception' contract) or stamp the SERVED model_id into the JudgmentRecord and add a `served_mismatch` flag to metadata so the provenance never lies. Add a fake-client test returning a different `model` field and assert the mismatch is surfaced.

findings.title: `ai-crucible characterize` exits 0 even when every model fails and zero judgments are collected
findings.worst_realistic_consequence: Ollama is down / the host is wrong / every model tag is unservable: run_panel catches each model's exception, prints it, and continues; all_records ends empty, profiles ends empty. main() still writes a report (with empty profiles) and returns 0. An automation/CI gate or the dogfood-swarm harness that shells out to `ai-crucible characterize` and checks the exit code treats a run that measured NOTHING as success — silent data loss presented as a green pass, and a stale/empty characterization report can be consumed as if valid.
findings.description: The per-model try/except (`# one bad model must not sink the panel`) is reasonable for ONE bad model, but there is no aggregate check: main() has a single `return 0` and no nonzero path when profiles/all_records is empty. For a measurement instrument, 'collected zero judgments' is a failure, not a success. The CLI dispatcher (cli.py) faithfully forwards this exit code, so the green-on-nothing result propagates to the console-script / npm-launcher entry point that automation calls. This is in characterize/run.py (the target of the `characterize` dispatch), squarely the 'exit codes nonzero on failure?' concern called out for the models-cli/CLI domain.
findings.evidence:
args.out.write_text(json.dumps(report, indent=2, default=str), encoding="utf-8")
    seated = [m for m, p in profiles.items() if p.seat_decision.value == "seat"]
    print(f"\nseated: {seated}")
    ...
    print(f"report -> {args.out}")
    return 0   # <- always 0, even when profiles=={} / all_records=={}
findings.proposed_fix_sketch: After run_panel, if not profiles (or not all_records) emit a structured error (code/message/hint) to stderr and `return 1` (distinct from the dispatcher's `2` for unknown-command). Optionally a non-fatal warning + nonzero when some-but-not-all models failed. Add a CLI test with a panel whose only model raises, asserting a nonzero exit.

findings.title: CLI usage banner hardcodes "v0.2.0" while --version reads it from package metadata (drift on next bump)
findings.worst_realistic_consequence: On the next version bump, `pyproject.toml`/`__init__.__version__`/the installed metadata move forward but the `_USAGE` string still says 'research preview, v0.2.0'. `ai-crucible --help` then reports a stale version that disagrees with `ai-crucible --version`. Cosmetic/honesty drift in the front-door banner; no measurement impact. Currently consistent (all 0.2.0), so this is a latent maintenance trap, not a present bug.
findings.description: _version() correctly resolves from importlib.metadata with a source-tree fallback, but the help banner embeds the literal 'v0.2.0' instead of interpolating _version(). Two sources of truth for the same fact in the same file.
findings.proposed_fix_sketch: Interpolate _version() into the banner (e.g. build _USAGE in a function or use an f-string at print time) so --help and --version cannot disagree.

domains.coverage_notes:
Read in full: claude_adapter.py, ollama_adapter.py, models/__init__.py, cli.py, __main__.py, src/ai_crucible/__init__.py, tests/test_models.py, tests/test_cli.py. Also read the dependencies that make the domain judgable: scoring/judge_panel.py (the family-exclusion mechanism the adapters feed), characterize/run.py (the CLI dispatch target + the -models/family path), characterize/types.py, and cross-checked research-grounding.md §3/§10.2/§11.5/§13/§587 and pyproject.toml.

Could not RUN the suites: scipy/anthropic/ollama/httpx are not installed in this environment (collection fails at scoring/stats.py importing scipy). All findings are from static analysis; this is a read-only audit so behavior was traced by hand against the fake-client tests, which faithfully model each adapter's client shape.

FAMILY TAGGING (the CRITICAL EXTERNAL_VERIFIER axis) verdict: ClaudeModel.family is a fixed class attribute \"claude\" and as_judge() stamps it correctly — CLEAN. OllamaModel.family is caller-supplied and correctly carried through as_judge()/pin_metadata()/JudgmentRecord; the DEFAULT_PANEL is correctly tagged. The ONE real weakening is the CLI `--models` path defaulting to the colliding literal \"unknown\" (models-cli-001) — fail-open via a shared real string rather than the documented None contract.

Sealed boundary (Tier-3 chrome) in the adapters: BOTH _judge_messages build only from attempt.messages + attempt.output and never read attempt.chrome — CLEAN. Note a test-coverage gap (not a defect): test_judge_does_not_read_chrome exercises only the Ollama judge path; there is no equivalent assertion for the ClaudeModel judge path (the code is identical and correct, but the Claude seal is unproven by a test).

ollama_adapter error handling (the 78%-covered, depended-on adapter): connection failure / model-not-found surface as exceptions (httpx raise_for_status; ImportError fallthrough) which matches the stated 'exception for the caller's andon' contract — acceptable. The material gap is served!=requested model detection (models-cli-002). No retry/backoff exists in either adapter, but the design explicitly delegates andon to the kernel, so absence-of-retry is a design choice, not a defect. Logprob→confidence math (_logprob_of rejects bool, _logprob_to_probability clamps to [0,1]) is correct and well-tested; the only soft spot is that exp(first-token logprob) measures only the FIRST sub-token of a multi-token verdict like 'PASS' (P|ASS) — this mis-estimates ECE confidence, but ECE is reported (not a pass/fail flip) and the simplification is documented in §12, so it is at most LOW and I did not file it.

claude_adapter: API error handling / rate-limit / retry are intentionally absent (kernel owns andon, documented) — not a defect. _extract_text returns \"\" for non-text blocks (tool_use etc.), masking a non-text-only response as an empty completion; documented as 'empty completion is a valid answer', so LOW/by-design — not filed.

No finding rises to CRITICAL in this domain. models-cli-001 (HIGH) is the closest to the instrument-lies class but is gated behind operator misuse of the non-default --models path plus a later scoring-time exclusion run; the default DEFAULT_PANEL path is safe.

findings.title: Coverage badge claims 96% but actual is 91.5%, and the CI gate floor is only 60%
findings.worst_realistic_consequence: A reader (or the dogfood-swarm shipcheck scorecard, which is required to reflect ACTUAL gate results not estimates) trusts a 96% coverage badge that overstates the real 91.5% figure by ~4.5 points. Because the only enforced gate is pyproject `fail_under = 60`, nothing in CI ties the badge to reality, so the number can drift arbitrarily far from the truth without any test going red. This is a self-measuring research instrument whose entire credibility rests on honest provenance; an inflated badge on the front door is exactly the kind of unbacked claim the shipcheck honesty gate exists to catch.
findings.description: The README coverage badge hard-codes `coverage-96%` while the audited actual coverage is 91.5%. The CI workflow (.github/workflows/ci.yml lines 61-72) derives its `--cov-fail-under` from `pyproject.toml [tool.coverage.report].fail_under`, which is 60 — so CI passes at any coverage >=60% and never validates the 96% badge claim. The badge is a static shields.io URL, not generated from a coverage run, so it is a hand-maintained number that has drifted above the real value. Note this is a documentation/honesty issue, not a broken test; the test SUITE itself is sound. Per the project's shipcheck rule 'Scorecard reflects ACTUAL gate results from shipcheck audit, not estimates', the badge should match the measured number.
findings.evidence: README.md:13: pyproject.toml:78: fail_under = 60 .github/workflows/ci.yml (covfloor step): floor=$(uv run python -c "... ['tool']['coverage']['report']['fail_under']") --cov-fail-under=${{ steps.covfloor.outputs.floor }}
findings.proposed_fix_sketch: Either (a) regenerate the badge from the real measured coverage (91% / 92%) so it matches, or (b) wire a coverage-badge step that writes the actual number after the CI cov run, and raise pyproject `fail_under` to a floor that actually protects the claim (e.g. 90) so the badge cannot silently drift above the gate again.

findings.title: The real-run driver in characterize/run.py (collect_records / run_panel / main) is untested, including a measurement-biasing parse-fail branch
findings.worst_realistic_consequence: The judge-admission characterization run — the pipeline that turns model outputs into the SEAT/SCREEN/REJECT decisions and the composed cross-family panel the instrument scores with — has no test on its grading loop. In `collect_records`, an unparseable judge verdict (`parsed is None`) is silently scored as the WRONG answer (`pred_num = 1 - gold_num`) rather than recorded as an abstention. A judge whose output format the regex misses thus has its measured objective_accuracy / Cohen's kappa / alt-test omega biased downward, which can flip its seat decision (REJECT a judge that is actually fine, or distort the reliability weights that drive the weighted panel verdict). Because run.py sits at 44% coverage and `collect_records`/`run_panel`/`main` have zero tests, this measurement-distorting behavior could change or break and the suite (relied on as the 473-test gate) would stay green — a gate that proves nothing about the path that produces the actual measurements.
findings.description: tests/test_characterize_run.py only exercises the PURE helpers (_parse_models, parse_choice, _to_num, _jury, known_groups_report, panel_correlation_report, irt_prune_report, panel_composition_report). The measurement-bearing functions collect_records (the per-item k-rerun grading loop), run_panel (the two-pass load->judge->evict->profile orchestrator with the human-labels vs circular model-jury branch at lines 232-258), perturbation_report, calibration_report, and main (the report assembler + --write-panel/--human-labels integration) have NO direct test. The most consequential untested line is the parse-failure-to-wrong-answer collapse at run.py:144 (`pred_num = _to_num(parsed) if parsed is not None else (1 - gold_num)`). Treating a malformed/unparseable verdict as a confident wrong answer (rather than dropping it or flagging it) is a measurement-integrity decision that should have a test pinning the intended behavior; right now nothing characterizes it. This is the coverage blind-spot the audit prompt flagged (characterize/run.py 44%), and it matters because these are the lines that produce the instrument's outputs, not cosmetic glue.
findings.evidence:
src/ai_crucible/characterize/run.py:140-148 (collect_records, untested):
        for ri in range(k):
            rec = await model.judge_item(item.prompt, run_index=ri)
            rec.item_id = item.id
            parsed = parse_choice(str(rec.predicted), gold_str)
            pred_num = _to_num(parsed) if parsed is not None else (1 - gold_num)
            rec.predicted = pred_num
            rec.gold = gold_num
            rec.correct = parsed == gold_str

(grep confirms only `irt_prune_report` and `cli.main` are referenced from tests; collect_records / run_panel / def main / perturbation_report / calibration_report appear in NO test file.)
findings.proposed_fix_sketch: Add tests that drive collect_records with a fake OllamaModel returning (a) a clean parseable verdict, (b) an unparseable verdict — and assert the intended handling explicitly (decide and pin: is an unparseable output a wrong answer or an abstention? right now it is silently scored wrong). Add a thin run_panel/main test using an injected fake panel (monkeypatch OllamaModel) over the sample items to cover the orchestration, the model-jury vs human-labels branch, and the report assembly + --write-panel path. This lifts the 44% blind spot on the path that produces the actual measurements.

findings.title: Symlink-escape security tests skip on Windows without SeCreateSymbolicLinkPrivilege
findings.worst_realistic_consequence: The two sandbox symlink-escape containment tests (read and write) are the proof that a symlink planted inside the workdir cannot be used to read/write outside the §10.4 sealed boundary. On a Windows developer machine without symlink privilege they pytest.skip, so a developer running the suite locally on Windows sees them pass-as-skipped and could believe the symlink boundary is verified when it was never exercised. The behavior is still covered on ubuntu-latest CI (where symlinks always create), so this is a local-visibility gap rather than an unguarded boundary — the escape is also defended-by-construction via Path.resolve() containment. Hence LOW, not HIGH.
findings.description: _make_symlink_or_skip calls pytest.skip when os.symlink raises (Windows without Developer Mode/admin). The skip is honest and documented, and the primary CI runner is ubuntu-latest (per github-actions rule), where the tests run. This is not a masked bug — the containment is structural — but it does mean the symlink-escape security property is unverified on the Windows dev rig this repo is developed on, with no compensating assertion. Worth noting because the prompt explicitly asks about OS-specific skips that could mask real bugs; here the underlying behavior is sound but the local proof is absent.
findings.evidence:
tests/test_sandbox.py:263-268:
    try:
        os.symlink(target, link)
    except (OSError, NotImplementedError) as exc:  # pragma: no cover - env-dependent
        pytest.skip(f"cannot create symlinks on this runner: {exc}")
    if not link.is_symlink():  # pragma: no cover - defensive
        pytest.skip("symlink creation did not produce a symlink on this runner")
findings.proposed_fix_sketch: Either gate CI to guarantee the symlink tests run on at least one runner (already true via ubuntu-latest) and document that in the test, or add a non-symlink unit test of the containment helper (_resolve_within) that exercises the same rejection path on every OS so the security property has an always-running proof independent of symlink-creation privilege.

domains.coverage_notes:
Read all 17 test modules in tests/ plus conftest.py and the sample puzzle fixture (tests/fixtures/puzzles/sample/meta.json, prompt, setup_script). Cross-read the relevant sources to judge whether tests prove what they claim: scoring/stats.py, scoring/judge_panel.py, calibration/known_groups.py, characterize/run.py, and key behaviors in kernel/engagement/attestation/budget/sandbox/roles/models.

VERDICT: The test suite is unusually rigorous and the test-integrity domain is largely CLEAN. I did NOT find vacuous tests (no mock-returns-what-mock-was-told tautologies), and I did NOT find half-invariant gate tests lacking a red-proving counterpart. Randomness is seeded (random.Random(20)); the only real time.sleep usage (test_sandbox timeout/process-tree tests) is intentional proof-by-elapsed-time with generous CI-safe ceilings, not flakiness.

Could not run the suite directly: the local interpreter is Python 3.14.5, outside the repo's '>=3.11,<3.14' constraint, so `pip install -e .` and pytest both fail to import ai_crucible. findings.title: README coverage badge claims 96% but actual coverage is 91.5%
findings.worst_realistic_consequence: An operator/reviewer assessing the instrument's test rigor trusts a stated 96% line coverage when the real number from `pytest --cov` is 91.50%. The badge is a green, hardcoded shields.io image (not coverage-service-driven), so it can never self-correct and overstates rigor by ~4.5 points on the project's most-read honesty surface. Not verdict-flipping math, hence MEDIUM, but it is a confirmed false numeric claim on the front door.
findings.description: The README header badge hardcodes `coverage-96%25` with alt text 'Coverage 96%'. Running the suite the CI/verify path runs (`uv run pytest --cov=ai_crucible`) reports TOTAL 91.50% across 473 passing tests. 96% appears nowhere in the actual measurement; it is the only coverage number on any honesty surface (SCORECARD/SHIP_GATE/CHANGELOG cite the 60% floor, not a headline coverage figure) and it is wrong. Correct value: 91% (91.50%).
findings.evidence: README.md:13 --- actual, from `uv run pytest --cov=ai_crucible` --- TOTAL 3081 262 91% Required test coverage of 60.0% reached. Total coverage: 91.50% ============================ 473 passed in 20.35s =============================
findings.proposed_fix_sketch: Claim is 'coverage 96%'; correct value is 91% (91.50% measured). Update the badge src+alt to `coverage-91%25` / 'Coverage 91%' (coordinator-owned prose change — flag, do not rewrite).

findings.title: SECURITY.md §1 describes the Solver container as a shipped, hardened Docker container; only a local-subprocess sandbox actually ships
findings.worst_realistic_consequence: An operator reading the threat model believes the Solver runs in a hardened, network-isolated, read-only, cap-dropped, digest-pinned Docker container TODAY, and configures/trusts the instrument accordingly — when the only shipped provider is `LocalSandbox`, a local subprocess with path-confinement and no network/seccomp/cap-drop enforcement at all. The doc's own honest-residual-risk note ('a plain Linux container is not a hard boundary') reinforces the false impression that a container exists. This over-trust of an unshipped containment layer is exactly the over-trust SECURITY.md says it exists to prevent. It is not a boundary breach (the real lock is the out-of-band oracle, which IS architecturally sound and shipped), so MEDIUM not HIGH.
findings.description: SECURITY.md §1 states in present tense that the Solver 'runs in a locked container' that 'is hardened: digest-pinned per-puzzle image, cap_drop: ALL, default seccomp, no-new-privileges, non-root, read_only rootfs, network_mode: none', and §No-telemetry repeats 'Solver containers run network_mode: none'. The shipped code (sandbox.py) only provides `LocalSandbox` (subprocess), and its OWN docstring is explicit that 'The hardened Docker / Cloud-Hypervisor microVM provider is a later wave' and that those properties belong to 'a future container's' provider. CHANGELOG Notes confirm 'the hardened container/microVM sandbox provider ... deferred to Phase 2+'. The SECURITY.md present-tense framing contradicts the code and the CHANGELOG. README.md:41 compounds this by describing the sandbox module as a 'locked, network-less container'.
findings.evidence:
SECURITY.md:19-22
The Solver runs in a locked container reached only through a narrow
`exec` / `read_file` / `write_file` channel (no raw shell). The container is
hardened: digest-pinned per-puzzle image, `cap_drop: ALL`, default seccomp,
`no-new-privileges`, non-root, `read_only` rootfs, `network_mode: none`.

--- contradicted by sandbox.py:6 ---
hook. The hardened Docker / Cloud-Hypervisor microVM provider is a later wave; the
--- and CHANGELOG.md:45-47 ---
  cross-family local-model panel (Phase 2), the hardened container/microVM sandbox
  provider, ... are deferred to Phase 2+ and are documented as such.
findings.proposed_fix_sketch: Claim is 'the container is hardened: digest-pinned ... read_only rootfs, network_mode: none' (present tense). Correct value: the shipped provider is a local subprocess (LocalSandbox); the hardened container is Phase-2+. Reword §1 + the no-telemetry bullet + README.md:41 to future/conditional tense ('the Phase-2 container provider will run network_mode: none') — coordinator-owned prose; flag only.

findings.title: SHIP_GATE.md and SCORECARD.md classify the repo as 'not a CLI / not an npm package'; the repo ships both a CLI and an npm package
findings.worst_realistic_consequence: The ship gate's hard-gate-B/C/D evaluation SKIPs every [cli] and [npm] item ('SKIP: library, no CLI entry point', 'SKIP: not an npm package') on the stated basis that the repo has no CLI/npm surface. But pyproject declares `[project.scripts] ai-crucible = ...`, src/ai_crucible/cli.py is a full argparse CLI with --help/--version/exit-codes 0/2, __main__.py backs `python -m ai_crucible`, npm/package.json publishes `@dogfood-lab/ai-crucible` with a bin, and release.yml builds+ships PyInstaller binaries and npm-publishes. So gate items that SHOULD have been actively verified ([cli] exit codes 0/1/2/3, [cli] --help accurate, [npm] npm pack dry-run, [npm] lockfile) were skipped under a false premise — the gate certifies 'done' for surfaces it never checked. A gate that passes by mis-declaring its own subject proves less than it claims. MEDIUM (not HIGH) because the CLI/npm surfaces do appear functional on inspection; the defect is the gate's integrity, not a shipped runtime bug.
findings.description: SHIP_GATE.md line 8 asserts 'This repo is: a Python library/framework. It is not a CLI, MCP server, desktop, or npm package — those tagged items are SKIPped with reasons.' Lines 30, 41, 52 then SKIP the [cli] exit-codes, [cli] --help, and [npm] npm-pack items as N/A. SCORECARD.md line 6 likewise tags it '(Python library/framework — not CLI/MCP/desktop/npm)'. Reality: pyproject.toml:40-41 `[project.scripts] ai-crucible = "ai_crucible.cli:main"`; cli.py implements --help/--version and returns exit code 2 on unknown command; __main__.py is the documented `python -m ai_crucible` / PyInstaller entry; npm/package.json:2 name `@dogfood-lab/ai-crucible` with bin/ai-crucible.js; release.yml smoke-tests `dist/<binary> --version` and runs `npm publish --provenance`. The cli.py header itself calls it 'the ai-crucible CLI — the unified entry point'. The classification used to gate the release is false, and it caused real gate items to be skipped rather than verified.
findings.evidence:
SHIP_GATE.md:8
**This repo is:** a Python **library/framework** (`[all]` + `[pypi]`). It is not a CLI, MCP server, desktop, or npm package — those tagged items are SKIPped with reasons.
SHIP_GATE.md:30
- [ ] `[cli]` Exit codes: 0/1/2/3 — SKIP: library, no CLI entry point.
SHIP_GATE.md:52
- [ ] `[npm]` `npm pack --dry-run` — SKIP: not an npm package.

--- contradicted by pyproject.toml:40-41 ---
[project.scripts]
ai-crucible = "ai_crucible.cli:main"
--- and cli.py:1 ---
"""ai-crucible CLI — the unified entry point.
--- and npm/package.json:2,10-12 ---
  "name": "@dogfood-lab/ai-crucible",
  "bin": { "ai-crucible": "bin/ai-crucible.js" },
findings.proposed_fix_sketch: Claim is 'not a CLI ... or npm package'; correct value is '[all]+[pypi]+[cli]+[npm]'. The [cli] and [npm] gate rows should be actively checked (exit codes via cli.py, --help text, `npm pack --dry-run` on npm/, lockfile), not SKIPped. Coordinator-owned: re-tag the repo type in SHIP_GATE.md:8 + SCORECARD.md:6 and re-evaluate the wrongly-skipped rows.

domains.coverage_notes:
Audited all in-scope files: .github/workflows/ci.yml, pages.yml, release.yml; pyproject.toml; verify.sh; .gitignore; and honesty surfaces README.md, SCORECARD.md, SHIP_GATE.md, SECURITY.md, CHANGELOG.md. Cross-checked claims against the actual code (sandbox.py, cli.py, __init__.py, npm/package.json), the lockfile (uv.lock), a real `uv run pytest --cov` run, and a real `uv build`.

CI / paths-gating (org rule) — CLEAN. ci.yml uses on.push.paths + on.pull_request.paths filters (src/**, tests/**, pyproject.toml, .github/workflows/**), has workflow_dispatch fallback, concurrency group with cancel-in-progress: true, runner ubuntu-latest, matrix is 1 OS × 2 Python versions (3.11/3.12 — within the ≤6-job / 2-3 versions rule), permissions: contents: read. The coverage floor is read from pyproject via tomllib (single source of truth — no drift between CI and verify.sh). pages.yml is paths-gated to site/** and is the accepted Pages exception. release.yml fires on release:published + workflow_dispatch only (no push trigger), concurrency cancel-in-progress: false (correct — never cancel an in-flight publish), and has version-matches-tag guards for BOTH pyproject (job pypi) and npm/package.json (job npm). All third-party actions are SHA-pinned with version comments; Node-24… note: pages.yml and release.yml pin actions/setup-node to node-version 22, not 24 — this is a config choice, not a drift defect (the org Node-24 cutover concerned GitHub-Actions runner Node, i.e. the action pins, which ARE updated to v6/v8 SHAs; the setup-node `node-version: 22` selects the JS runtime for the site build/npm publish and is independently valid).

Workflow-file count: 3 files (ci/pages/release) vs the org 'max 2' rule — NOT flagged as drift because release.yml's header comment explicitly claims pages.yml as the accepted GitHub-Pages exception and ci+release as the org CI/publish pair; cross-OS binary matrix in release.yml is the documented explicit-request exception for the npm binary launcher. These are acknowledged in-doc, so consistent.

Supply-chain — CLEAN. [tool.uv] constraint-dependencies floors (aiohttp>=3.14.1, starlette>=1.3.1) are NOT stale: uv.lock pins aiohttp 3.14.1 and starlette 1.3.1, and the constraints are recorded in the lockfile's constraint list (lines 12-13). pip-audit step audits the RESOLVED runtime deps via `uv export --no-emit-project --no-hashes` (transitive deps included, dev/stats excluded) — correct scope for Ship Gate D. requirements-audit.txt scratch is gitignored. No unpinned action drift. Wheel packaging scope ([tool.hatch.build.targets.wheel] packages=src/ai_crucible) means the git-tracked puzzles/seed-sulzbach-55252/oracle/ANSWER_KEY_*.txt is NOT bundled into the PyPI wheel or the PyInstaller binary — no answer-key leak via published artifacts. (The answer key being committed to the repo at all is an oracle/eval-integrity-domain concern, out of my lane — noting, not claiming.)

Version-matches-tag — CLEAN at the source-of-truth level: pyproject 0.2.0 == __init__.__version__ 0.2.0 == npm/package.json 0.2.0 == SHIP_GATE/SCORECARD/CHANGELOG '0.2.0'. No v0.2.0 git tag has been cut yet (SHIP_GATE D row honestly SKIPs version-matches-tag as 'no release tag cut'), and release.yml enforces the match at publish time for both pypi and npm jobs.

Development Status :: 3 - Alpha (pyproject classifier) vs README 'research preview (v0.2.x)' — CONSISTENT, not a conflict: 'Alpha' and 'research preview / pre-1.0' describe the same maturity; no contradiction to flag.

FINDINGS (4): (001) README coverage badge 96% vs actual 91.50% — confirmed false number, MEDIUM. (002) SECURITY.md §1 + README.md:41 describe a shipped hardened Docker container; only LocalSandbox subprocess ships (contradicted by sandbox.py docstring + CHANGELOG 'Phase 2+') — over-trust risk, MEDIUM. (003) SHIP_GATE.md:8 + SCORECARD.md:6 mis-classify the repo as 'not a CLI / not an npm package' though it ships both ([project.scripts], cli.py, __main__.py, npm/package.json bin, release.yml binary+npm publish) — caused [cli]/[npm] gate rows to be SKIPped under a false premise, gate-integrity issue, MEDIUM. (004) SHIP_GATE.md:54 wheel named 'crucible-0.2.0' vs actual 'ai_crucible-0.2.0' — cosmetic, LOW.

No CRITICAL/HIGH found in this domain. The CI math/gating, supply-chain pins, and version-tag plumbing are sound; nothing here flips a measurement verdict, leaks the oracle, or bypasses an andon/budget. The HIGH-bar items (oracle leak, sealed-boundary bleed, scoring-math errors, same-family judge inclusion) live in code domains outside ci-supply-docs and were not in scope. All four findings are claims-vs-code/claims-vs-claims drift on honesty surfaces; per domain instructions, fixes name the exact claim + correct value rather than proposing prose rewrites (docs are coordinator-authored).
